bio.title: Dr.
bio.bio_blurb: Harvard professor of obstetrics for over twenty years

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard professor of obstetrics; physician in Boston

narrative_blocks.header: A classicist pressed into the family trade
narrative_blocks.body: Reynolds excelled in the classics at Boston Latin and taught there two years before his father pressed him into Harvard Medical School. He took to obstetrics, and held the Harvard chair in the subject for more than two decades until 1885.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a granddaughter of Paul Revere
narrative_blocks.body: In 1859 Reynolds married Jane Minot Revere, granddaughter of the Revolutionary silversmith. The match folded the Revere line into a family already two generations deep in Boston medicine, with his father the founding surgeon of the eye-and-ear infirmary.

narrative_blocks.header: His own grandson couldn’t name his colleagues
narrative_blocks.body: A book of his medical correspondence passed to a grandson who, in 1913, wrote the hospital librarian that he could not vouch for the handwritten list of names inside or recognize the hand. The doctor’s working world had grown unreadable within two generations.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, who ran the Revere copper works

bio.title: Dr.
bio.bio_blurb: Harvard professor of obstetrics for over twenty years

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard professor of obstetrics; physician in Boston

narrative_blocks.header: A classicist pressed into the family trade
narrative_blocks.body: Reynolds excelled in the classics at Boston Latin and taught there two years before his father pressed him into Harvard Medical School. He took to obstetrics, and held the Harvard chair in the subject for more than two decades until 1885.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a granddaughter of Paul Revere
narrative_blocks.body: In 1859 Reynolds married Jane Minot Revere, granddaughter of the Revolutionary silversmith. The match folded the Revere line into a family already two generations deep in Boston medicine, with his father the founding surgeon of the eye-and-ear infirmary.

narrative_blocks.header: His own grandson couldn’t name his colleagues
narrative_blocks.body: A book of his medical correspondence passed to a grandson who, in 1913, wrote the hospital librarian that he could not vouch for the handwritten list of names inside or recognize the hand. The doctor’s working world had grown unreadable within two generations.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, who ran the Revere copper works

bio.bio_blurb: Granddaughter of Paul Revere; Boston matriarch

narrative_blocks.header: Through her, the Revere name entered Boston medicine
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Minot Revere was a granddaughter of Paul Revere and daughter of the copper magnate Joseph Warren Revere. Her 1859 marriage to Dr. John Phillips Reynolds braided the Revere line into three generations of Boston physicians.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son bought back the Revere house
narrative_blocks.body: Among Jane’s eight children was John Phillips Reynolds Jr., who in 1902 purchased and preserved her grandfather’s house in Boston’s North End. The Revere ancestry she carried became the cause that defined his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Her name carried two Boston dynasties
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Minot Revere bore the names of two old Boston families, the Minots and the Reveres, at the moment they joined the medical Reynoldses. Her children’s names, Paul Revere Reynolds and Mary Robbins Reynolds among them, kept the ancestry deliberately visible.

bio.title: Dr.
bio.bio_blurb: Boston obstetrician and gynecologist

narrative_blocks.header: The fourth Reynolds to take up medicine
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Reynolds followed his father, grandfather, and great-grandfather into Boston medicine, practicing as an obstetrician and gynecologist. The line had begun with the founding surgeon of the eye-and-ear infirmary three generations earlier.

bio.title: BG
bio.bio_blurb: Army general; Harvard administrative vice president

narrative_blocks.header: He ran the Army’s medical supply in WWII
narrative_blocks.body: As a brigadier general, Reynolds served as chief of the Army Medical Supply Service in the Office of the Surgeon General from 1942 to 1945. He had charge of medical supplies across the wartime force.

narrative_blocks.header: From utility president to Harvard’s back office
narrative_blocks.body: After the war and a stint as president of Columbia Gas and Electric, Reynolds became administrative vice president of Harvard from 1946 to 1960. He brought a general’s logistics to the running of the university.

bio.title: Capt.
bio.bio_blurb: Harvard artillery officer who died in France, 1919

narrative_blocks.header: A Harvard gunner lost just after the armistice
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Parker Reynolds, Harvard class of 1918, served as an operations officer with the 101st Field Artillery in the 26th “Yankee” Division. He died in Lorraine in January 1919, after the armistice, and lies in the Saint-Mihiel American Cemetery.

bio.bio_blurb: Reynolds daughter; mother of the Strong-Burr line
Subject of a Frank Benson portrait, c.1909

bio.title: Jr.
bio.bio_blurb: Financier who saved the Paul Revere House, 1902

notable.notable_blurb: Saved the Paul Revere House in 1902

career.organization: Suncook Mills, Submarine Signal Co., Peter Bent Brigham Hospital, and others

narrative_blocks.header: He bought his ancestor’s house to save it
narrative_blocks.body: In 1902 Reynolds, a great-great-grandson of Paul Revere, purchased the decaying Revere house in the North End and formed an association to restore it as a museum. It survives as the oldest building in downtown Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the letters of the abolitionists
narrative_blocks.body: As grandnephew of Wendell Phillips, Reynolds preserved Phillips’s collection of letters from the leading abolitionists, Susan B. Anthony, Garrison, Emerson, Lydia Maria Child, and others. The correspondence is now catalogued at the Harvard Library.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried Revere money to a Union spy
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1880s and ’90s Reynolds traveled to Richmond to deliver money from the Revere family to the aging Union spy Elizabeth Van Lew, who had cared for two imprisoned Revere cousins. After her death he marked her grave with a boulder from the Massachusetts State House.

narrative_blocks.header: He raced a Kaiser and built a galleon
narrative_blocks.body: A passionate yachtsman, Reynolds raced the German Kaiser at Kiel and the young King of Spain at Bilbao in 1907, then published his account anonymously as "The Cruise of the Golden Eagle." His Maine vacation house, designed to resemble a Spanish galleon, still stands as a coastal landmark.

narrative_blocks.header: He moved a museum and shunned the credit
narrative_blocks.body: Among the financiers who relocated the Museum of Fine Arts to Huntington Avenue and built the Copley Plaza on its old site, Reynolds preferred to organize ventures without taking visible charge. He disliked being photographed, and pictures of him are scarce.

cross_connections.display_label: his great-great-grandfather, whose house he bought and preserved

bio.bio_blurb: Founded the first literary agency in America

narrative_blocks.header: He invented the American literary agent
narrative_blocks.body: In 1893 Reynolds opened the first literary agency in the United States, a profession that did not yet exist on this side of the Atlantic. He brokered the work of authors who had no one to represent them to publishers.

narrative_blocks.header: His client list reads like a syllabus
narrative_blocks.body: Reynolds represented Stephen Crane, George Bernard Shaw, Leo Tolstoy, Joseph Conrad, Stephen Leacock, and P. G. Wodehouse, among others. His longest-running client was the novelist Arthur Stringer.

narrative_blocks.header: Two patriot names met in one man
narrative_blocks.body: A great-grandson of Paul Revere and nephew of Wendell Phillips, Reynolds carried the names of both the midnight rider and the abolitionist into the business of letters. His own son joined the agency and ran it after him.

bio.title: Jr.
bio.bio_blurb: Literary agent who ran the family agency

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited America’s first literary agency
narrative_blocks.body: Paul Jr. joined his father’s pioneering agency in 1923 and later became its president, continuing to represent leading British and American authors. The agency’s records now fill nearly three hundred boxes at Columbia.

bio.bio_blurb: Reynolds daughter; wife of a Boston neurologist
Reynolds son; Brookline and Milton family man
Reynolds daughter who married into Virginia
Linguist; matriarch of a Harvard academic dynasty

narrative_blocks.header: Her marriage seeded a Harvard dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: A linguist by training, Theresa Reynolds married the mathematician Julian Lowell Coolidge in 1901 and bore seven children. Among them were a Fogg Museum director and a daughter who married Boston’s leading cultural historian.

bio.bio_blurb: Harvard mathematician; first master of Lowell House

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard geometer; first master of Lowell House

narrative_blocks.header: He taught a future president at Groton
narrative_blocks.body: Before joining Harvard, Coolidge taught at Groton, where one of his pupils was Franklin Delano Roosevelt; the two remained friends for life. He went on to spend his whole career at Harvard, chairing its mathematics department.

narrative_blocks.header: He held Oxford’s first science degree
narrative_blocks.body: After Harvard, Coolidge took the very first Bachelor of Science ever awarded by Oxford, in 1897, then a doctorate at Bonn under Eduard Study. His treatises on the circle, the sphere, and non-Euclidean geometry became standards.

narrative_blocks.header: He built Lowell House from raw brick
narrative_blocks.body: Named the first master of Lowell House in 1930, Coolidge had to invent the residential culture of a building “new and raw from the builders’ hands.” His vision shaped Harvard’s house system for generations.

narrative_blocks.header: A geometer who commanded two thousand men
narrative_blocks.body: During the First World War, Coolidge served as a major and liaison officer to the French General Staff, with two thousand American men under his command in Paris. France made him a chevalier of the Legion of Honor in 1919.

narrative_blocks.header: In retirement he wrote mathematics’ history
narrative_blocks.body: After retiring in 1940, Coolidge turned to the history of his field, publishing studies of geometrical methods and "The Mathematics of Great Amateurs." He was a record-setting miler in his Oxford days and a lifelong oarsman.

cross_connections.display_label: a fellow eminent American mathematics professor of the era

bio.bio_blurb: Hotchkiss School educator; Navy veteran

narrative_blocks.header: He shared a name with a famous uncle
narrative_blocks.body: A Hotchkiss schoolmaster for twenty years and a Navy officer in the Second World War, Archibald Cary Coolidge lived to ninety-nine. His second wife was the children’s author Olivia Coolidge, and he carried the name of his great-uncle, Harvard’s first library director.

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of Julian and Theresa Coolidge
Director of Harvard’s Fogg Museum for twenty years

notable.notable_blurb: Fogg Museum director; MFA Boston president
notable.primary_url_label: American Academy of Arts and Sciences

narrative_blocks.header: He gave up architecture for its history
narrative_blocks.body: Coolidge moved to New York to become an architect, then decided he lacked the talent and switched to architectural history under the émigré scholars Panofsky and Krautheimer. His first book on the Lowell mill town became a classic of American social history.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty years steering the Fogg
narrative_blocks.body: Named director of Harvard’s Fogg Art Museum at thirty-four, Coolidge ran it for two decades, strengthening its Islamic and modern holdings and adding the museum’s first modernist works. He treated the museum as a laboratory for training future curators.

narrative_blocks.header: He roller-skated between his train commutes
narrative_blocks.body: A co-founder of the Society of Architectural Historians, Coolidge later presided over the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston. He was known to roller-skate between stations on his Princeton-to-New-York commute.

cross_connections.display_label: gave his entire art collection to the Fogg, which this man directed

bio.title: Dr.
bio.bio_blurb: Co-founder of the Massachusetts Eye and Ear Infirmary

notable.notable_blurb: Founded the Massachusetts Eye and Ear Infirmary, 1824

education.school_name: Studied under Dr. John Collins Warren

narrative_blocks.header: He brought European eye surgery home to Boston
narrative_blocks.body: After training under John Collins Warren, Reynolds crossed to London to study the eye under William Lawrence and surgery under Astley Cooper and Abernethy, then to Paris under Bichat and Dupuytren. He returned among the first Americans equipped to practice the new ophthalmic surgery.

narrative_blocks.header: His infirmary still runs two centuries on
narrative_blocks.body: In 1824 Reynolds and John Jeffries opened a charity eye clinic that became the Massachusetts Eye and Ear Infirmary, the first of its kind in the country and now past its bicentennial. It made the diseases of the eye and ear a recognized American specialty.

narrative_blocks.header: Seventy years of practice, one cased portrait
narrative_blocks.body: Reynolds practiced for more than seventy years, long enough that the colleagues of his prime had nearly all died before him. He survives in a single daguerreotype of the Society for Medical Improvement, the twelve Boston physicians who met to dissect each other’s hardest cases.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the first Boston mayor’s daughter
narrative_blocks.body: In 1825 Reynolds wed Margaret Wendell Phillips, daughter of John Phillips, Boston’s first mayor. Her younger brother Wendell Phillips would become the era’s most famous abolitionist orator, a connection Reynolds married into at the height of his surgical fame.

cross_connections.display_label: who likewise founded a Boston specialty clinic and a medical library

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of Boston’s first mayor; Reynolds matriarch

narrative_blocks.header: Her brother was abolition’s loudest voice
narrative_blocks.body: Margaret married into the Reynolds medical family, but her younger brother Wendell Phillips became the most celebrated abolitionist orator in America. Their father, John Phillips, was the first mayor of Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: She linked a mayor’s house to a surgeon’s
narrative_blocks.body: Daughter of Boston’s first mayor, Margaret married the founding surgeon of the Massachusetts Eye and Ear Infirmary. Their son carried both legacies into a Harvard professorship in obstetrics.

bio.bio_blurb: Boston Reynolds who bridged to a medical dynasty

narrative_blocks.header: His son turned the family toward medicine
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Reynolds married Deborah Belcher in 1790 and lived to eighty-seven in Boston. His son became the founding ophthalmic surgeon of the Massachusetts Eye and Ear Infirmary, beginning four generations of Boston physicians.

bio.bio_blurb: First mayor of Boston; Massachusetts Senate president

narrative_blocks.header: Boston’s first mayor was a compromise choice
narrative_blocks.body: When Boston incorporated as a city in 1822, rivals Harrison Gray Otis and Josiah Quincy deadlocked, and their supporters settled on John Phillips. Ill health led him to decline a second term, and he died twenty-eight days after leaving office.

narrative_blocks.header: He fathered a mayor and an abolitionist
narrative_blocks.body: Through his daughter Margaret, Phillips became grandfather to the Boston Reynolds physicians; his son Wendell Phillips grew into the most famous abolitionist orator of the age. He had eight children with Sally Walley.

narrative_blocks.header: A mayor who sat on Harvard’s governing board
narrative_blocks.body: Before the mayoralty, Phillips was elected to the American Academy of Arts and Sciences and the American Antiquarian Society, and joined the Harvard Corporation in 1812. He gave the city’s Fourth of July oration in 1794, at twenty-three.

cross_connections.display_label: the first mayor of New York, as Phillips was of Boston

burialCemetery.name: Mount Auburn Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic garden cemetery, Cambridge MA. Plot: Azalea Garden, Lot 11002, Mausoleum 20.

INST133.name: Boston Latin School
INST133.notes: America's oldest public school. In-law connection through Samuel Parker Hinckley (father of I00974; Harvard A.B. 1871; 'fitted for college at Boston Latin School'). Confirmed Hooker-tree alumni: HD2066 Frederick William Atherton and HD2304 John Noyes Colby (class of 1912). In-law connection: I00974's father Samuel Parker Hinckley (Harvard A.B. 1871). | Merged from INST059: John Noyes Colby (HD2304) class of 1912 — the largest senior class in school history to that point (102 members). Received award for excellence in modern studies Jun 4, 1908. Class Day exercises Apr 24, 1912.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
